blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the real-time counting, the ranking, and the serving — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: trending is a *real-time, approximate, global ranking* problem. We have to watch a firehose of engagement events, decide what's "spiking right now," and serve a tiny ranked list to hundreds of millions of people in milliseconds — while the underlying truth is changing every second. Freshness fights accuracy fights cost.

🏢 **Company lens:** I'll target an **Amazon SDE-3** bar — so I'll make **cost and operations** (alarms, runbooks, on-call) first-class, work backwards from what the user actually sees, and lean on **Bias for Action**: ship the approximate-but-good-enough counting path now, and scope exact analytics as a separate bet. Tell me if you'd rather I tilt Meta-style (pure read hot-path) and I'll re-emphasize.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Ingest engagement signals in real time.** The system should consume likes, comments, shares, and views on posts as they happen.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Every time someone likes or shares a post, we need to hear about it within a few seconds so we can tell what's catching fire right now."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the input is a firehose — millions of events a second, bursty, and we can't lose so many that the ranking goes wrong, but we also can't afford to count every single one exactly.

**FR2 — Detect what's trending (compute a trend score).** The system should turn raw counts into a "trending score" that reflects *velocity* (how fast engagement is rising), not just total volume.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A post with a million old likes isn't trending — a post that just got 50 thousand likes in ten minutes is. We measure the spike, not the all-time total."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we need a rate-of-change over a sliding time window across billions of posts, and we have to discount established giants and detect genuine acceleration.

**FR3 — Rank and produce the trending list.** The system should produce an ordered Top-N (global, and optionally per-topic / per-region) list of trending posts.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Out of everything happening, give me the 50 hottest items in the right order, refreshed every minute or so."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** ranking billions of candidates down to 50 cheaply, repeatedly, without recomputing the world each time.

**FR4 — Serve the trending list to users with very low latency.** The system should return the trending list to any user's feed/sidebar in a few milliseconds.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When you open Facebook, the trending box should already be there — no spinner."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the same small list is read by hundreds of millions of people — a massive read fan-out on a hot key.

**Below the line (out of scope for now):** personalized trending per individual user, full-text trend search, spam/bot filtering depth (I'll touch it as a deep dive), monetization/ads ranking, comment-tree rendering, and the post-creation/storage system itself (we assume posts already exist with IDs).

### Non-Functional

**NFR1 — Freshness.** Trending should reflect the world within ~1–2 minutes (near-real-time, not instant).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If something blows up at 3:00, it should show in trending by about 3:02."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** trending shows yesterday's news — users lose trust and the feature is pointless; we'd be beaten by Twitter every time.

**NFR2 — Read latency.** p99 of serving the list ≤ ~50ms at the edge.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 out of 100 times the trending box loads in under a twentieth of a second."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the box janks the whole feed load; users perceive the entire app as slow.

**NFR3 — Availability (read path).** ~99.99% ("four nines") on serving; trending is non-critical, so it must *degrade*, never hard-fail.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Down less than about an hour a year — and even when the counting breaks, we still show a slightly stale list instead of an error."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a broken trending widget takes down or blanks the home feed for everyone.

**NFR4 — Scale.** Handle ~1–5M engagement events/sec ingest and ~1M+ reads/sec of the list.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Millions of clicks coming in per second, millions of people reading the list per second."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the ingest pipeline backs up, scores lag by hours, and freshness (NFR1) is dead.

**NFR5 — Cost-efficiency.** Approximate counting is acceptable; we should not pay to count every event exactly.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Getting it 99% right for a tenth of the price is the right call — nobody audits the exact like-count behind trending."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** exact counting at this firehose costs an order of magnitude more in compute and storage for no user-visible benefit.

**NFR6 — Consistency stance.** Eventual consistency is fine; the list is inherently approximate and read-mostly.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Two users might see the list one slot different for a few seconds — totally fine."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** we'd force strong consistency on a ranking that changes every second — enormous cost for zero benefit.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** bot/spam manipulation can poison trending; I'll handle it as a deep dive rather than pretend it's free. And "trending" is partly an editorial/policy decision (hate, misinformation) — I'll note the safety-filter hook but treat policy as out of scope.

> 💬 "The whole game here is freshness vs. cost vs. read-scale. I'll lean on approximate counting to win cost, a streaming pipeline to win freshness, and a pre-computed cached list to win read latency."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is a real-time ranking problem. We ingest a firehose of engagement — likes, comments, shares, views — score posts by how fast they're spiking rather than their all-time totals, rank down to a Top-N list, and serve that tiny list to hundreds of millions of people in milliseconds. My non-functionals are freshness in a minute or two, fifty-millisecond reads, four nines on serving with graceful degradation, and crucially — this can be approximate, so I'll spend my budget on freshness and read-scale, not on counting every event exactly."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**1. Is trending global, or sliced by topic/region/language?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It changes how many lists I maintain and shard — one global Top-N is trivial to cache; thousands of per-topic lists is a different storage story."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If YES (sliced) → I maintain many leaderboards keyed by (region, topic) and need a routing/aggregation layer. If NO (one global list) → a single hot key I can cache everywhere trivially.
- **Assumption:** one global list + a handful of regional lists (US, EU, IN, …). I'll design for the sliced case since global is a degenerate subset.

**2. What's the freshness requirement — seconds or minutes?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Sub-second changes my whole architecture to pure streaming with no batch fallback; a minute or two lets me use cheap micro-batch windows."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If seconds → Flink/streaming with tight windows, more compute. If minutes → 1-minute tumbling windows, much cheaper.
- **Assumption:** ~1–2 minute freshness. Good enough for "what's hot," far cheaper.

**3. Does trending need to be exact, or is approximate fine?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If approximate is fine I can use probabilistic counters and sampling and cut cost 10x; if exact, I need full aggregation."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If approximate → Count-Min Sketch / HyperLogLog, sampling. If exact → full event aggregation, much more expensive.
- **Assumption:** approximate. The ranking only needs relative order to be roughly right.

**4. What counts as a "signal," and are signals weighted?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "A share is worth more than a view; the weights drive the scoring formula and which event streams I even consume."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If weighted → score = w1·likes + w2·comments + w3·shares + … and I need all streams. If unweighted → simple count, one stream.
- **Assumption:** weighted (share > comment > like > view), weights tunable by config, not code.

**5. How big is the list (Top-N) and how often does it refresh?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Top-50 refreshed each minute is a tiny cached blob; Top-10,000 personalized is a serving system of its own."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If small N → one cached JSON blob per region. If large/personalized → a ranking service per request.
- **Assumption:** Top-50 per list, refreshed every ~60s.

**6. Do we need decay so old spikes fade?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Without time-decay, yesterday's viral post lingers forever; decay is what makes 'trending' mean 'now.'"
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If YES → exponential time-decay on scores. If NO → fixed sliding window only.
- **Assumption:** YES — sliding window + exponential decay.

**7. How much do we care about bot/manipulation resistance?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Trending is a prime manipulation target; if it matters I need rate-limiting, dedup, and anomaly detection in the path."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If YES → per-user dedup, velocity caps, anomaly scoring before counting. If NO → count raw.
- **Assumption:** basic defense in scope (dedup + per-user caps), deep ML detection out of scope.

**8. Personalized or one-size-fits-all?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Personalization turns a shared cached list into a per-user compute problem — totally different scale."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If personalized → candidate generation + per-user ranking. If global → shared cached list.
- **Assumption:** global/regional, not per-user. (Personalization is the obvious extension in §11.)

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume a sliced-but-mostly-global, approximate, ~1-minute-fresh Top-50 with time decay and light bot defense. Before I size it — does that match what you had in mind, or do you want strict freshness or personalization in scope?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

Glossing as I go. **DAU** = daily active users. **QPS** = queries per second, requests hitting the server each second. **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DAU | 3B users × ~70% active = 2.1B | ~2B | "two billion daily users" | scale of read fan-out |
| Engagement events/sec | 2B DAU × ~50 engagements/day = 100B/day; 100B ÷ 86,400s ≈ 1.16M/s; peak ×3 ≈ 3.5M/s | ~1M avg, ~3.5M peak | "a few million events a second" | ingest must be a stream, not synchronous DB writes |
| Read QPS (trending list) | 2B DAU × ~5 feed-opens/day = 10B/day; 10B ÷ 86,400 ≈ 116K/s; peak ×3 ≈ 350K/s; if each feed-open reads trending = same | ~120K avg, ~350K peak | "a few hundred thousand reads a second" | a single hot key → must serve from cache/CDN, never origin DB |
| Active posts in window | ~1M new posts/min × 60-min window ≈ 60M candidate posts | ~60M | "tens of millions of live candidates" | can't sort 60M every minute naively → top-K heap + sketches |
| Counter state | 60M posts × ~50 bytes (id + counts + score) ≈ 3GB; ×3 replication ≈ 9GB | ~10GB | "single-digit GB of hot counters" | fits in memory → Redis/in-process, no disk on hot path |
| Trending list payload | Top-50 × ~1KB/post metadata = 50KB per region × ~20 regions = 1MB total | ~1MB | "a megabyte of list data total" | trivially cacheable everywhere — the read problem is fan-out, not size |

**The ONE number that forces the design:** ~**350K reads/sec on a single hot list**. A single Redis node tops out around ~100–150K ops/s, so at 350K we cannot serve reads from one node — we **replicate the list to read replicas + edge/CDN cache**. The flip threshold: below ~100K/s a single cache node is fine; above it, replicate/CDN.

🎙️ **Script:** "Two numbers drive everything. Ingest is a few million events a second at peak — way too much for synchronous database writes, so it has to be a stream. And reads are a few hundred thousand a second, all hammering one tiny list — so I serve that from a replicated cache or even the CDN, never from origin. Storage is a non-issue, the hot counters are single-digit gigabytes that fit in memory."

## 4. Core Entities

🎙️ This is a first draft of the entities — I'll add full field definitions in the data model (§7).

**Post** — the content being ranked; owns its id, author, topic/region, creation time. It's the thing that trends.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of it like a contestant in a race — we don't store much about it here, just enough to identify it and show it in the list."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Where does post metadata for the list come from?" → we store only post_id in the leaderboard and hydrate title/thumbnail from a separate post-metadata cache at serve time.

**EngagementEvent** — one user action (like/comment/share/view) on a post; owns user_id, post_id, type, timestamp. The raw fuel.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a single vote dropped into a ballot box — tiny, disposable, only meaningful in aggregate."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Do you store every event forever?" → no — we aggregate into counters and drop raw events to a cheap data lake for later/audit, not the hot path.

**PostCounter (windowed)** — the rolling aggregate per post: weighted counts within the current time window. Owns post_id → bucketed counts.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a tally sheet on a fridge that resets each hour — it tracks how many times something happened recently, not ever."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you do a sliding window cheaply?" → bucketed counters (e.g. per-minute buckets) summed over the window, with old buckets expiring.

**TrendScore** — the computed velocity+decay score per post; the sort key. Owns post_id → float score, updated_at.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a stock price — a single number that goes up when interest accelerates and drifts down when it cools."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Total likes or rate?" → rate of change over the window with exponential decay, so old giants don't dominate.

**TrendingList (Leaderboard)** — the materialized Top-N per (region, topic). Owns the ordered list + version/timestamp.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like the Billboard Top 50 — a small ranked chart we publish and everyone reads."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How is it kept ordered?" → a Redis Sorted Set (score-ordered) per slice, or a periodically materialized blob.

🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are Post, the raw EngagementEvent, a windowed PostCounter that tallies recent activity, a TrendScore that turns those tallies into one velocity number, and the TrendingList — the published Top-50. The surprising one is that the EngagementEvent is basically disposable — we never keep it on the hot path, we fold it into counters and throw the raw stream into cheap cold storage. I'll add the field types and stores in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

**GET /v1/trending** — read the trending list. GET because it's a pure read, cacheable, idempotent.
- **Key request fields:** `region: string` (defaults to geo-IP) — which regional list; `topic: string?` (optional) — filter to a topic; `limit: int` (default 50, capped server-side) — page size. The client does **not** send a score or ordering — the server owns ranking so it can't be gamed.
- **Concrete example:**
```
// Request
GET /v1/trending?region=US&topic=sports&limit=20

// Response 200
{
  "version": "2026-06-19T14:32:00Z",
  "items": [
    { "postId": "p_991", "rank": 1, "score": 8421.5, "title": "...", "thumb": "..." },
    { "postId": "p_774", "rank": 2, "score": 7980.0, "title": "...", "thumb": "..." }
  ]
}
```
- **One key design decision:** serve a **versioned, fully-materialized blob** (not a live query). The `version` lets the CDN/client cache it and dedup; if the worker is down, we keep serving the last good version (graceful degradation).
- 🗣️ "This is just a read — I keep it a plain cacheable GET so the CDN can absorb the hundreds of thousands of reads a second, and I stamp a version so clients and caches know when it changed."

**POST /v1/events** (internal, high-volume) — ingest an engagement event. POST because it records new state. Usually this is fed by existing engagement services, not the public client.
- **Key request fields:** `postId: string` — what was engaged; `userId: string` — for dedup/abuse caps (taken from the authenticated session, **never trusted from the body**); `type: enum(like|comment|share|view)` — drives the weight; `ts: server-set` — the server stamps time, the client can't forge it.
- **Concrete example:**
```
// Request (internal, fire-and-forget to the stream)
POST /v1/events
{ "postId": "p_991", "type": "share" }
// userId comes from the auth context; ts is server-set

// Response 202
{ "accepted": true }
```
- **One key design decision:** returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — we drop the event onto a stream (Kafka) and return immediately; the caller doesn't wait for counting. Idempotency: a natural key `(userId, postId, type)` within a short window dedups double-fires.
- 🗣️ "Writes are fire-and-forget onto a log — I return 202 the instant the event is durably queued, because the caller should never block on our analytics. And I take the user id from the session, not the request body, so nobody can stuff the ballot under someone else's name."

**Security:** the caller is identified from session/JWT (JWT = a signed token proving who you are). We never trust client-supplied user ids, timestamps, or scores — the server stamps time and computes score from the DB so the list can't be tampered with.

🎙️ **Script:** "Two endpoints. A cacheable GET for the list — versioned so caches and clients can dedup and so I can fall back to the last good copy. And an internal POST for events that returns 202 immediately because it just drops onto a durable log; the user id and timestamp are server-set so the counts can't be forged."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Ingest engagement signals in real time" (FR1)

We need to absorb a few million events a second without losing the firehose and without blocking callers.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Event stream (Kafka) | A durable, replayable log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | We need to decouple ingest spikes (3.5M/s) from processing; Kafka buffers and replays | Not direct DB writes (3.5M writes/s melts any DB); not RabbitMQ (lower throughput, weaker replay) | A few seconds of pipeline lag; eventual, not instant |
| Ingest API / gateway | A thin stateless service that validates and drops events onto Kafka | Stateless so we scale it horizontally behind a load balancer | Not writing from clients directly to Kafka (security, no auth/validation) | Extra hop, but cheap |

🎙️ **Narration:** "Engagement comes in faster than any database can take it, so the first thing I do is put a durable log — Kafka — between the world and my processing. Kafka's just an append-only log that holds events until my consumers catch up; if a consumer dies I replay from where it left off, so I don't lose data. I rejected writing straight to a database — millions of writes a second would melt it — and I rejected RabbitMQ because I want Kafka's replay and raw throughput. The trade-off is a few seconds of lag, which is fine for a one-minute freshness target."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, partitioned, replayable commit log. Here it buffers the engagement firehose so spikes don't overwhelm processing and nothing is lost on consumer failure.
> **Partition** — a shard of a Kafka topic that gives ordering and parallelism. Here we partition by post_id so all events for one post land on one consumer and counting is consistent.

**Diagram — ingest path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client / Engagement svc]
    Client -->|"1: POST event"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    subgraph Ingest["Ingest path"]
        GW -->|"2: validate + auth"| GW2[Validated]
        GW2 -->|"3: produce, partition by post_id"| K[[Kafka topic]]
    end
    K -.->|"4: buffered for consumers"| Cons[Stream processors]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,GW2,Cons svc
    class K queue
```
↳ reuses existing: none (first slice).

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **POST event** — client/engagement service fires the event. *Why here:* the gateway is the only authenticated entry point. *State:* nothing yet. *What if it fails:* client retries; dedup key prevents double-count.
2. **Validate + auth** — gateway checks the session, applies per-user velocity caps. *Why:* stops obvious bot floods before they enter the system. *State:* event tagged with trusted user_id. *What if it fails:* reject with 429, event dropped (acceptable — it's one signal).
3. **Produce to Kafka, partitioned by post_id** — event appended to the log. *Why:* same post → same partition → consistent counting. *State:* event durably persisted, replicated 3x. *What if it fails:* gateway returns 5xx, caller retries; Kafka's replication means a broker loss doesn't lose data.
4. **Buffered for consumers** — events sit until stream processors pull them. *State:* backlog absorbs spikes. *What if it fails:* consumers lag, freshness degrades gracefully — list gets a bit stale, never wrong.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "The key move is putting a durable log between the firehose and my counting — that's what lets a 3.5-million-a-second spike not take anything down."

🎙️ **Script:** "Ingest is all about not drowning. A thin stateless gateway authenticates and drops each event onto Kafka, partitioned by post_id so every event for a given post is counted in one place. Kafka buffers spikes and replays on failure, so I trade a couple seconds of lag for never losing the firehose."

### 6.2 "Detect what's trending — compute a trend score" (FR2)

Now we turn the raw stream into per-post windowed counts and a velocity score.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stream processor (Flink) | An engine that reads the Kafka stream and maintains running, windowed aggregates in memory | We need sliding-window counts + decay over millions of events/sec with exactly-once state | Not a cron batch job (too slow for 1-min freshness); not raw consumers (we'd re-implement windowing/state) | Operational complexity of a stateful streaming cluster |
| Count-Min Sketch | A tiny probabilistic counter that estimates counts for millions of keys in fixed memory | 60M posts in single-digit GB; exact per-post counters cost far more | Not exact hashmaps (memory blows up under hot keys); accuracy is approximate (fine per NFR5) | Slight over-count on collisions — acceptable for ranking |
| Score store (Redis Sorted Set) | An in-memory store that keeps members ordered by a score so Top-N is O(log n) | We need cheap "give me the top 50 by score" repeatedly | Not sorting a DB table each minute (scan 60M rows); not in-app heap only (not shared across nodes) | In-memory cost; needs replication for durability |

🎙️ **Narration:** "Flink reads the Kafka log and keeps a running tally per post over a sliding window — say the last 30–60 minutes in one-minute buckets — applying weights so a share counts more than a view and exponential decay so old spikes fade. To keep memory tiny across tens of millions of posts I use a Count-Min Sketch — a probabilistic counter that trades a sliver of accuracy for fixed memory. The resulting scores go into a Redis Sorted Set, which keeps posts ordered by score so pulling the Top-50 is dirt cheap. I rejected a periodic batch job — too slow for one-minute freshness — and exact counters — too much memory for no real benefit."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Flink** — a stateful stream-processing engine. Here it maintains sliding-window weighted counts and decay over the event stream with exactly-once guarantees.
> **Count-Min Sketch (CMS)** — a probabilistic data structure that estimates how many times each key was seen using fixed memory. Here it counts engagements for 60M posts in a few GB instead of an exact map.
> **Sliding window** — counting only events in the recent past (e.g. last hour) by summing per-minute buckets and expiring old ones. Here it's what makes "trending" mean "now."
> **Exponential decay** — multiplying older counts by a shrinking factor so recent activity weighs more. Here it lets a fast riser overtake a stale giant.
> **Redis Sorted Set** — an in-memory set whose members are kept ordered by a numeric score. Here it stores post_id→score so Top-N reads are O(log n).

**Diagram — scoring path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka topic]]
    K -->|"1: consume events"| F[Flink processor]
    subgraph Scoring["Scoring path"]
        F -->|"2: update CMS windowed counts"| CMS[Count-Min Sketch state]
        CMS -->|"3: compute velocity + decay"| SC[Score computed]
    end
    SC -->|"4: ZADD post_id score"| ZS[(Redis Sorted Set)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class F,SC svc
    class K queue
    class CMS,ZS db
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1).

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **Consume events** — Flink pulls from Kafka per partition. *Why:* partitioned by post_id means each post's state lives on one operator. *State:* in-memory window updated. *What if it fails:* Flink checkpoints state; on crash it restores and replays from last offset (exactly-once).
2. **Update CMS windowed counts** — increment the post's weighted count in the current minute bucket. *Why:* fixed memory across 60M posts. *State:* sketch reflects recent activity. *What if it fails:* slight over-count, never under-count — ranking still roughly right.
3. **Compute velocity + decay** — score = weighted recent rate × decay(age). *Why:* velocity, not total, is "trending." *State:* a fresh score per active post. *What if it fails:* stale score → post lingers, corrected next window.
4. **ZADD into Sorted Set** — write post_id→score. *Why:* keeps the leaderboard ordered for cheap Top-N. *State:* leaderboard reflects newest scores. *What if it fails:* retry; Sorted Set is replicated.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "The insight is that 'trending' = rate of change, not total — so I count in a sliding window with decay, and I use a sketch so 60 million counters fit in a few gigs."

🎙️ **Script:** "This is the brain. Flink reads the stream and keeps a sliding-window, weighted count per post — shares worth more than views, recent worth more than old via exponential decay. I store those counts in a Count-Min Sketch so tens of millions of posts fit in a few gigabytes, then I push each post's score into a Redis Sorted Set so the Top-50 is a cheap ordered read. The trade-off is approximation, but for a ranking nobody benefits from exactness."

### 6.3 "Rank and produce the trending list" (FR3)

We periodically materialize the ordered list from the Sorted Set so reads don't pay ranking cost.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Materializer worker | A small scheduled job that reads Top-N from the Sorted Set, hydrates metadata, and writes a versioned blob | Pre-computing once a minute is far cheaper than ranking per read | Not ranking on every read (350K/s × sort = waste); not client-side ranking (can't trust/scale) | List is up to ~60s stale (matches NFR1) |
| Metadata cache | An in-memory store of post_id→{title, thumb, author} | We store only ids in the leaderboard; hydrate cheaply at materialize time | Not joining the posts DB per read (slow, hot) | Slightly stale metadata, fine for display |

🎙️ **Narration:** "A tiny worker runs every ~60 seconds: it asks the Sorted Set for the Top-50 per region/topic, looks up titles and thumbnails from a metadata cache, and writes a single versioned JSON blob. That way the expensive ranking and hydration happen once a minute, not once per read. I rejected ranking on every request — that's hundreds of thousands of sorts a second for an identical answer."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Materialized view** — a precomputed, stored result of a query. Here it's the published Top-50 blob, refreshed each minute so reads are O(1).
> **Hydration** — replacing ids with full display objects. Here the worker turns post_ids into titles/thumbnails before publishing.

**Diagram — ranking/materialize path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Cron[Scheduler ~60s]
    Cron -->|"1: trigger"| W[Materializer worker]
    subgraph Rank["Ranking path"]
        W -->|"2: ZREVRANGE top 50"| ZS[(Redis Sorted Set)]
        W -->|"3: hydrate metadata"| MC[(Metadata cache)]
    end
    W -->|"4: write versioned blob"| LB[(Trending list cache)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class W svc
    class Cron queue
    class ZS,MC,LB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Redis Sorted Set (from §6.2).

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **Trigger** — scheduler fires every ~60s per slice. *Why:* matches freshness target cheaply. *State:* none. *What if it fails:* last blob keeps serving (graceful).
2. **ZREVRANGE top 50** — pull highest-scored posts. *Why:* Sorted Set makes this O(log n + 50). *State:* candidate ids in hand. *What if it fails:* retry; serve old blob meanwhile.
3. **Hydrate metadata** — fetch titles/thumbs. *Why:* leaderboard holds only ids. *State:* full display objects. *What if it fails:* show id-only / skip missing, don't block the list.
4. **Write versioned blob** — publish new list with timestamp. *Why:* atomic swap, cacheable. *State:* new version live. *What if it fails:* swap is atomic — readers see old or new, never partial.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "I rank once a minute into a frozen blob — readers never pay ranking cost, they just grab the latest published list."

🎙️ **Script:** "Ranking is decoupled from reading. A worker wakes every minute, pulls the Top-50 from the Sorted Set, hydrates titles and thumbnails, and publishes a versioned blob. Reads then just fetch that blob — the ranking cost is paid once per minute instead of hundreds of thousands of times a second."

### 6.4 "Serve the trending list with low latency" (FR4)

The published blob must reach hundreds of thousands of readers a second in milliseconds.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN / edge cache | Servers near users that cache the blob so reads never reach origin | 350K reads/s of one tiny versioned blob is the textbook CDN case | Not origin-only (one cache node tops ~150K/s); not per-user compute (no need) | Up to ~60s edge staleness (matches freshness) |
| Read replicas (Redis) | Copies of the cache for cache-miss/origin reads | Absorb misses and CDN refreshes without one node melting | Not single node (350K > one node's ceiling) | Replica lag of milliseconds |

🎙️ **Narration:** "The list is one tiny versioned blob, so I push it to the CDN with a ~60-second TTL. The CDN absorbs essentially all of the hundreds of thousands of reads a second at the edge; origin only sees a trickle of refreshes, served from Redis read replicas. This is the cleanest part — a small, shared, slowly-changing object is exactly what CDNs are for."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a network of edge servers near users that cache content. Here it serves the trending blob from the edge so origin never sees the read storm.
> **Read replica** — a read-only copy of a datastore. Here replicas serve cache-miss/refresh reads so no single node hits its ops ceiling.

**Diagram — serve path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    User[User feed]
    User -->|"1: GET /trending"| CDN[CDN edge cache]
    subgraph Serve["Serve path"]
        CDN -->|"2: hit, return blob"| User
        CDN -.->|"3: miss / TTL refresh"| RR[(Redis read replica)]
    end
    RR -.->|"4: read latest blob"| LB[(Trending list cache)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class User client
    class CDN svc
    class RR,LB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Trending list cache (from §6.3).

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **GET /trending** — user's feed requests the list. *Why:* served at the edge nearest them. *State:* none. *What if it fails:* client shows cached/last list.
2. **Hit, return blob** — CDN serves cached blob. *Why:* ~99% hit rate on a slow-changing shared object. *State:* none. *What if it fails:* fall to step 3.
3. **Miss / TTL refresh** — CDN fetches from Redis replica. *Why:* offloads origin. *State:* edge cache refilled. *What if it fails:* serve stale edge copy.
4. **Read latest blob** — replica returns published list. *State:* none. *What if it fails:* serve last-known blob; never error the feed.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "One tiny shared object read by everyone is the perfect CDN job — the read 'scale problem' basically evaporates at the edge."

🎙️ **Script:** "Serving is the easy half. The list is a single small versioned blob, so I cache it at the CDN with a one-minute TTL. The edge absorbs the entire read storm; origin Redis replicas only see refreshes. Worst case the user gets a list that's a minute old — exactly within my freshness budget."

**Final (high-level)** — the reconciled union of all slices:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client / feed]
    Client -->|"write: POST event"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    Client -->|"read: GET trending"| CDN[CDN edge]
    subgraph Write["Write / Async path"]
        GW -->|"produce"| K[[Kafka]]
        K -->|"consume"| F[Flink processor]
        F -->|"counts"| CMS[(Count-Min Sketch)]
        F -->|"ZADD score"| ZS[(Redis Sorted Set)]
    end
    subgraph Read["Read path"]
        Cron[Scheduler] --> W[Materializer]
        W -->|"top 50"| ZS
        W -->|"hydrate"| MC[(Metadata cache)]
        W -->|"publish blob"| LB[(Trending list cache)]
        CDN -.->|"miss"| RR[(Redis replica)] -.-> LB
    end
    K -.->|"raw archive"| DL[(Data lake)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,F,W,CDN svc
    class K queue
    class CMS,ZS,MC,LB,RR,DL db
```
Load-bearing schema: Redis Sorted Set keyed `(region,topic)` → member post_id, score float; Trending list cache → versioned JSON blob per slice.

🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: on the **write/async path**, events flow through the gateway into Kafka, Flink folds them into windowed sketches and pushes velocity scores into a Sorted Set — and the raw stream also dumps to a cheap data lake for audit. On the **read path**, a per-minute worker materializes the Top-50 into a versioned blob, and the CDN serves that blob to everyone, hitting Redis replicas only on a miss. The two halves meet at the Sorted Set."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. Want me to go deep on the real-time counting and approximation, the hot-key serving, or the bot-manipulation problem?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — Entity table**

| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Post (metadata) | post_id (PK), author_id, topic, region, created_at | Posts DB (existing) + metadata cache | post_id | eventual (display) |
| EngagementEvent | event_id, user_id, post_id, type, ts | Kafka (transient) → Data lake (cold) | post_id (Kafka partition) | at-least-once |
| PostCounter | post_id (PK), bucketed weighted counts | Flink state / Count-Min Sketch | post_id | approximate |
| TrendScore | post_id (PK), score, updated_at | Redis Sorted Set | (region, topic) | eventual |
| TrendingList | slice_key (PK), version, ordered items | Trending list cache + CDN | (region, topic) | eventual |

**PART B — Storage decision cards**

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: EngagementEvent ingest**
1. **What it is:** a durable, append-only log that holds a firehose of events and lets consumers read at their own pace and replay on failure.
2. **Why we picked it:** we must absorb ~3.5M events/sec and decouple ingest from counting; only a partitioned log buffers spikes and gives replay without losing data.
3. **Considered instead:** **RabbitMQ** — lower throughput and weaker replay at this scale; **direct DB writes** — 3.5M writes/s melts any DB; **Kinesis** — fine on AWS but Kafka gives us partition control and portability.
4. **Trade-off:** a few seconds of pipeline lag and the ops cost of running Kafka.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Engagement comes in faster than any database can take, so I put Kafka — a durable log — in front. It buffers spikes, replays on failure, and lets me partition by post so counting stays consistent. I skipped writing straight to a DB because millions of writes a second would melt it."

**🗄️ Redis (Sorted Set + cache) — used for: TrendScore, TrendingList, metadata**
1. **What it is:** an in-memory data store; a Sorted Set keeps members ordered by score so Top-N is cheap, and it also serves as a fast cache.
2. **Why we picked it:** we need "give me the top 50 by score" repeatedly and microsecond reads of a hot list; in-memory ordered structures are purpose-built for this.
3. **Considered instead:** **sort a SQL table each minute** — scanning 60M rows is wasteful; **Elasticsearch** — overkill, we don't need full-text; **in-process heap only** — not shared/durable across serving nodes.
4. **Trade-off:** memory cost and the need for replication/persistence for durability.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Scores and the published list live in Redis. The Sorted Set keeps posts ordered so the Top-50 is a cheap range read, and the same Redis serves the list as a hot cache. I considered re-sorting a SQL table every minute but scanning tens of millions of rows for an identical answer is a waste."

**🗄️ Data lake (S3 / object storage) — used for: raw EngagementEvent archive**
1. **What it is:** cheap, virtually unlimited blob storage for raw events we may want later.
2. **Why we picked it:** we don't keep raw events on the hot path, but we want them for audit, re-processing, and offline ML — object storage is the cheapest durable home.
3. **Considered instead:** **keep events in Kafka forever** — expensive; **a warehouse like Snowflake** — costlier for raw cold dumps; **drop events entirely** — loses audit/replay ability.
4. **Trade-off:** querying the lake is slow (minutes), but that's fine for offline use.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Raw events are disposable on the hot path, but I tee them into cheap object storage so I can re-process or audit later. I'm not paying warehouse prices to keep a firehose I rarely query."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Record engagement | Kafka | at-least-once | never lose the firehose; dedup handles duplicates |
| Update score | Redis Sorted Set | eventual/approximate | ranking only needs roughly-right order |
| Read trending list | CDN/Redis | eventual (≤60s) | a slightly stale shared list is fine |
| Read-your-own-write | n/a | not required | trending is global, not "did MY like land" |

🎙️ **Script:** "Storage splits cleanly by job. Kafka is the durable intake for the firehose, at-least-once because I'd rather double-count than lose. Redis does the heavy lifting on the hot path — a Sorted Set for ranking and a cache for the published list — and it's all eventual, which is exactly right for an approximate ranking. Raw events tee off to cheap object storage for audit and offline ML. I considered re-sorting a SQL table each minute but rejected it — scanning tens of millions of rows for the same Top-50 is pure waste."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the three forces — freshness, cost, read-scale — and reason which one the current bottleneck threatens.

### How do we count engagement at firehose scale without melting storage?

**#### Bad: Increment a DB row per event**
🗣️ **What this is:** "Every like does an UPDATE on a counter row in a database."
**Approach:** on each event, `UPDATE post_counters SET likes = likes+1 WHERE post_id=?`.
**Why people try this:** it's the obvious, exact, simplest thing.
**⚠️ What breaks:** at 3.5M events/sec that's 3.5M writes/sec, and hot posts create row-lock contention — thousands of writes serializing on one row. The DB's connection pool (the fixed set of open connections, typically 50–200) exhausts in seconds and everything stalls.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any viral post becomes a single hot row that no DB can sustain.

**#### Good: Stream + in-memory windowed counters**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad did a durable disk write per event on a hot row; Good moves counting into a stream processor's memory and batches durability.
🗣️ **What this is:** "We add events to a log and a fast in-memory engine tallies them in time windows."
**Approach:** Kafka + Flink keep per-post counts in memory over a sliding window, checkpointing state periodically. Reads come from memory, not disk.
**Why reasonable:** removes per-event disk writes and row locks.
**⚠️ What breaks:** exact in-memory maps for 60M posts blow memory under skew — a celebrity post's key gets huge, and GC pauses spike.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** memory cost and skew at tens of millions of keys.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka]] -->|"events"| F[Flink window state]
    F -->|"checkpoint"| CP[(Durable state)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class F svc
    class K queue
    class CP db
```

**#### Great: Probabilistic counters (Count-Min Sketch + HLL)**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good used exact maps that blow memory under skew; Great fixes memory with fixed-size probabilistic structures.
🗣️ **What this is:** "We use a clever tiny data structure that estimates counts for millions of items using a fixed, small amount of memory."
**Approach:** maintain a **Count-Min Sketch** (estimates per-key counts via hashing into a fixed 2-D array) per window for total engagement, and **HyperLogLog** (HLL — estimates the number of *unique* users with tiny memory) to count unique engagers and resist a single bot inflating a post. Scores derive from these estimates.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** exact = 60M posts × ~50B = ~3GB and grows with skew; CMS with 0.1% error ≈ tens of MB fixed regardless of key count — ~100x smaller and bounded.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Hash collision over-count | a cold post slightly inflated | width sized for <0.1% error; ranking unaffected |
| Flink operator crash | window state lost | checkpoint + replay from Kafka offset (exactly-once) |
| Hot-key skew | one partition overloaded | sub-partition hot posts by post_id+bucket, merge at read |
| Kafka lag | scores stale | freshness degrades gracefully, never wrong |
| Bot inflates count | fake trending | HLL unique-user count + per-user dedup caps |

🎙️ **Script:** "I count with probabilistic structures. A Count-Min Sketch estimates how many engagements each post got using fixed memory — tens of megabytes for tens of millions of posts instead of gigabytes — and a HyperLogLog counts unique engagers so one bot spamming likes can't fake a trend. They're approximate, but a trending ranking doesn't need exactness, and Flink checkpoints plus Kafka replay give me exactly-once recovery."
🧠 **If they ask "isn't approximation dangerous?":** "For ranking, only relative order matters, and the error is bounded under a tenth of a percent — far smaller than the noise in what 'trending' even means. If we ever needed exact counts we'd reconcile from the data lake offline."

### How do we serve a single hot list to 350K readers/sec?

**#### Bad: Query origin per request**
🗣️ **What this is:** "Every page load asks the database for the trending list."
**⚠️ What breaks:** 350K identical queries/sec on one origin; a single Redis node caps ~150K ops/s, so origin saturates and latency explodes.
**🔁 What forces upgrade:** one hot key over a single node's ceiling.

**#### Great: Materialize + CDN edge cache**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad recomputed/served from origin per read; Great precomputes once and serves from the edge.
🗣️ **What this is:** "We freeze the list into one small file and let servers near users hand it out."
**Approach:** materialize a versioned blob each minute; push to CDN with ~60s TTL; origin Redis replicas serve only misses/refreshes.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** with a ~99% CDN hit rate, origin sees 350K × 1% ≈ 3.5K/s — comfortably under one node's ceiling.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Materializer down | list stops updating | keep serving last good version (stale, not broken) |
| CDN edge miss storm | origin spike | request coalescing / single-flight at origin |
| Redis primary down | no fresh writes | replicas serve reads; failover promotes replica |
| Blob partially written | corrupt list | atomic version swap — readers see old or new only |

🎙️ **Script:** "Serving a hot shared object is the textbook CDN case. I materialize the Top-50 into one tiny versioned blob a minute and cache it at the edge with a one-minute TTL, so ~99% of reads never leave the edge and origin sees only a few thousand a second. If the materializer dies, I keep serving the last good version — stale, never an error."
🧠 **If they ask "what about the thundering-herd on TTL expiry?":** "Single-flight at origin — the first miss recomputes, everyone else waits for it, so the origin sees one refresh per slice per minute, not a stampede."

### How do we keep "trending" meaning "now" (windowing + decay)?

**#### Good: Fixed tumbling window**
🗣️ **What this is:** "Count engagement in fixed clock buckets, e.g. each hour."
**⚠️ What breaks:** boundary effects — a post hot at 1:59 looks dead at 2:00 when the bucket resets; jittery rankings.

**#### Great: Sliding window + exponential decay**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** tumbling windows reset abruptly; sliding+decay makes scores smooth and recency-weighted.
🗣️ **What this is:** "We always count the last N minutes and gently fade older activity so the chart moves smoothly."
**Approach:** per-minute buckets summed over a rolling window; multiply each bucket by decay = e^(−λ·age). Score = Σ(weight·count·decay).
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** with half-life ~30 min, a spike from 2 hours ago contributes ~6% of its original weight — old giants fade naturally.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Clock skew across nodes | mis-bucketed events | event-time (not processing-time) windows in Flink |
| Late-arriving events | missed from window | allowed-lateness grace period, then drop |
| Decay too aggressive | flickery list | tune λ via config, not code |

🎙️ **Script:** "Trending has to mean *now*, so I use a sliding window with exponential decay instead of fixed hourly buckets. Each minute's count fades on a roughly 30-minute half-life, so a fresh spike overtakes a stale giant smoothly — no jarring resets at clock boundaries. I window on event-time, not arrival-time, so out-of-order events still land in the right minute."
🧠 **If they ask "why not just total likes in the last hour?":** "That ignores acceleration — a post going from 0 to 10K in ten minutes should beat one that's drifted to 12K over the full hour. Decay encodes velocity."

### How do we resist bot/manipulation?

**#### Great: Dedup + unique-user + anomaly caps**
🗣️ **What this is:** "We count unique real people, not raw clicks, and flag suspicious spikes."
**Approach:** per-user dedup key `(user, post, type)` in a short-TTL set; per-user velocity caps at the gateway; HLL unique-engager count as the dominant score input; an anomaly check flags posts whose engagement comes from too few accounts or too-correlated IPs and quarantines them from the list.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Bot farm likes | fake trend | unique-user (HLL) dominates score; low unique → low score |
| Single user spam | inflated count | dedup key collapses repeats |
| Coordinated burst | brief fake spike | anomaly quarantine + human review hook |

🎙️ **Script:** "Trending is a manipulation magnet, so I score on *unique* engagers via HyperLogLog, not raw clicks — a thousand likes from ten accounts barely moves the needle. I dedup per user, cap velocity at the gateway, and quarantine posts whose engagement looks too concentrated for a human review hook. Deep ML detection is a separate system, but these cheap defenses kill the obvious attacks."
🧠 **If they ask "what about a real celebrity who's always hot?":** "Decay plus velocity handles it — they only trend when their rate *spikes*, not just because their totals are high."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets.** The **read path** targets four nines (~99.99% = ~52 min downtime/year). It's achieved with CDN edge caching, multi-AZ Redis replicas, and the rule that the read path always degrades to the last good blob rather than erroring. **Multi-AZ** = running copies in multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power another takes over automatically. The **write/scoring path** targets three nines (~99.9% = ~8.7 hrs/year) — if it lags, the list just gets stale, which is acceptable.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what user sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| CDN edge | reads hit origin | slightly slower list, still correct | replicas absorb; edge refills |
| Redis Sorted Set | no new scores | list freezes at last version (stale) | failover to replica, replay from Kafka |
| Flink | scoring stops | list stops updating, still served | restart from checkpoint + Kafka offset |
| Kafka | ingest stops | counts freeze; list stale | multi-broker replication; producers retry |
| Materializer | no new blobs | last good list served indefinitely | restart job; idempotent |
| Metadata cache | missing titles/thumbs | items show id/placeholder | hydrate from posts DB on recovery |

**9C — RPO / RTO.** **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if we crash right now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until we're working again.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engagement events | ~0 (Kafka 3x replicated) | seconds | broker replication + producer retry |
| Window/score state | ~1 checkpoint interval (sec) | <1 min | Flink checkpoint + Kafka replay |
| Trending list blob | ~1 min (rebuildable) | seconds | recompute from Sorted Set |
| Raw archive (lake) | ~0 | n/a (offline) | durable object storage |

Trending is rebuildable end-to-end from Kafka, so even a total Redis loss recovers by replaying — RPO on the *derived* data is effectively a checkpoint, not a disaster.

**9D — Cost breakdown.** Top drivers: (1) **CDN egress** — every byte served to readers is charged; at hundreds of thousands of reads/sec this is the biggest line item, mitigated by the list being ~1MB and highly cacheable. (2) **Flink/stream compute** — stateful processing of a few million events/sec; the single biggest *compute* cost, which is exactly why approximate counting (CMS/HLL) matters — it shrinks state ~100x. (3) **Kafka storage/throughput** — buffering and replicating the firehose. Rough order of magnitude: low-to-mid six figures/month. The biggest single optimization: **approximate counting + edge caching** — together they cut both the compute and egress bills dramatically.

🎙️ **Script:** "When things fail, the user almost never sees an error — they see a slightly older list. If Flink or Kafka hiccup, scores freeze and the list goes stale; if Redis fails over, we replay from Kafka and rebuild. The only true 'outage' would be the CDN and all replicas down at once, which is why the read path is multi-AZ and four-nines. Cost-wise, CDN egress and stream compute dominate, and approximation plus edge caching are what keep both in check."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Approximate counting (CMS/HLL) vs exact counters**
1. **Chose & why:** approximate — fixed, ~100x smaller memory at firehose scale.
2. **Gave up:** exact counts; small bounded error in scores.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We measure the crowd with a really good estimate instead of counting every single head — way cheaper, basically as accurate for ranking."
4. **When this reverses:** if trending counts ever became user-facing exact numbers or billing inputs, we'd reconcile from the data lake or count exactly.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I went approximate because ranking only needs roughly-right order, and it cuts memory and cost an order of magnitude."

**🔀 Decision: Eventual consistency / ~1-min freshness vs real-time strong**
1. **Chose & why:** eventual, ~1-min — lets me cache aggressively and batch ranking.
2. **Gave up:** sub-second freshness.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Trending is a minute behind reality, like a leaderboard that refreshes each minute — nobody notices, and it makes the whole thing cheap and fast to read."
4. **When this reverses:** if the product needed second-by-second 'breaking now' (e.g. live-event trending), I'd tighten Flink windows and skip the materialize/CDN layer for a push channel.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "A minute of staleness buys me edge caching and cheap ranking; for 'what's hot,' that's an easy trade."

**🔀 Decision: Pre-materialized blob + CDN vs rank-per-request**
1. **Chose & why:** pre-materialize — pay ranking once a minute, not 350K times a second.
2. **Gave up:** per-user personalization on the hot path.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We print one newspaper everyone reads, instead of writing a custom paper for each reader."
4. **When this reverses:** if we add per-user personalized trending, we move to candidate-generation + per-request ranking with a much bigger serving tier.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "One shared list is a CDN's dream; I only break it up if we need personalization."

**🔀 Decision: Stream (Kafka+Flink) vs batch (cron over logs)**
1. **Chose & why:** stream — needed for one-minute freshness.
2. **Gave up:** operational simplicity; a streaming cluster is more to run.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We process events as they arrive on a conveyor belt instead of collecting them and processing once an hour."
4. **When this reverses:** if freshness relaxed to hourly, a cheap batch job over the data lake would do — drop Flink entirely.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Streaming is the cost of freshness; if we only needed hourly trending I'd go back to a simple batch job."

🎙️ **Script:** "My least-certain calls are approximation, eventual freshness, and a shared pre-materialized list — and they all trace back to the same principle: trending is approximate and read-mostly, so spend on freshness and read-scale, not on exactness. Each one reverses cleanly: exact counts if it becomes billing, tighter windows if we need live trending, per-request ranking if we add personalization."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two posts are basically tied — how do you decide order, and is it stable?"** *(core algorithm)*
**Mechanism:** scores come from a Sorted Set ordered by the velocity+decay float; ties break by a secondary key (recency, then post_id) so order is deterministic. Because we re-rank each minute, near-ties may swap between versions — expected for an approximate list.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We sort by a hotness number; if two are equal we break the tie by who's newer, and the list refreshes each minute so tiny swaps are normal."
**💬 One-liner:** "Deterministic tie-break on recency, re-materialized each minute — small reshuffles are by design, not a bug."

**❓ "What happens when Redis crashes?"** *(failure)*
**Mechanism:** reads keep serving the last published blob from CDN/replicas (graceful degradation); a replica is promoted via failover, and scoring state rebuilds by replaying from Kafka, since the Sorted Set is derived data.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The list freezes at its last version instead of breaking, and we rebuild scores by replaying the event log."
**💬 One-liner:** "Redis is derived state — worst case the list goes stale for a minute while we replay Kafka."

**❓ "How do you handle a sudden 10x event spike during a live event?"** *(scale/hot-spot)*
**Mechanism:** Kafka absorbs the spike as buffer; Flink autoscales consumers; hot posts are sub-partitioned (post_id+bucket) so one celebrity post doesn't overload a single operator, merged at read.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The log soaks up the surge, and we split a viral post's counting across workers so no single machine chokes."
**💬 One-liner:** "Kafka buffers the surge and we sub-shard hot keys — no single operator owns a celebrity post."

**❓ "Can the same engagement be counted twice?"** *(consistency)*
**Mechanism:** Kafka is at-least-once, so duplicates are possible; a per-user dedup key `(user,post,type)` with a short TTL collapses repeats, and HLL counts unique users anyway, so duplicates barely move the score.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Maybe a duplicate sneaks in, but we dedup per user and count unique people, so it doesn't matter."
**💬 One-liner:** "At-least-once ingest, but dedup keys plus unique-user counting make double-counts harmless."

**❓ "How do you stop someone gaming trending with bots?"** *(security)*
**Mechanism:** score on unique engagers (HLL) not raw clicks, per-user velocity caps at the gateway, dedup, and an anomaly quarantine for engagement concentrated in too few accounts/correlated IPs.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "A thousand likes from ten fake accounts barely counts because we measure unique real people and flag suspicious bursts."
**💬 One-liner:** "Unique-user scoring plus velocity caps and anomaly quarantine kill the cheap attacks."

**❓ "How would someone forge a like as another user?"** *(security)*
**Mechanism:** user_id is taken from the authenticated session/JWT, never the request body; the gateway stamps the timestamp; events failing auth are dropped before Kafka.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "You can't say a like came from someone else — we read who you are from your login, not from what you send."
**💬 One-liner:** "Identity comes from the session, not the payload — forged user ids never enter the pipeline."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"** *(cost)*
**Mechanism:** CDN egress and Flink stream compute dominate; egress is tamed by the list being ~1MB and edge-cached at ~99% hit, and compute is tamed by approximate counting (CMS/HLL) shrinking state ~100x. Biggest further cut: longer CDN TTL if freshness can relax.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Serving bytes to everyone and crunching the event stream cost the most; caching one tiny list and estimating counts instead of counting exactly slash both."
**💬 One-liner:** "Egress and stream compute lead; edge caching and approximation are the two levers."

**❓ "How would you add per-user personalized trending?"** *(extensibility)*
**Mechanism:** keep the global leaderboard as candidate generation, then add a per-request ranking service that re-scores the Top-few-hundred against the user's interests/affinity — moving from one shared blob to a personalized read tier.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Use the global hot list as a shortlist, then re-rank it for each person by what they care about."
**💬 One-liner:** "Global list becomes the candidate set; a lightweight per-user re-ranker personalizes the top slice."

**❓ "How would you add per-topic and per-region trending?"** *(extensibility)*
**Mechanism:** key the Sorted Set and materialized blob by `(region, topic)`; events already carry topic/region, so it's just more leaderboards — storage is still single-digit GB.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We already tag each event with topic and region, so we just keep a separate small chart per slice."
**💬 One-liner:** "More leaderboards keyed by slice — the data's already tagged, so it's cheap."

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "Trending Posts is a real-time, approximate ranking of what's spiking right now, served to hundreds of millions of people. The design splits into two halves. The read half is dead simple: a worker materializes the Top-50 into one tiny versioned blob each minute, and the CDN serves that blob from the edge, so a few hundred thousand reads a second never touch origin. The write half is the hard part: a firehose of a few million engagement events a second flows through Kafka into Flink, which keeps sliding-window, decayed, weighted counts — and the key mechanism is probabilistic counting, a Count-Min Sketch plus HyperLogLog, which lets me count tens of millions of posts and unique engagers in a fraction of the memory while resisting bots. The two halves meet at a Redis Sorted Set that keeps posts ordered by score. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: trending is approximate and read-mostly, so I spend my budget on freshness and read-scale, never on counting every event exactly."
